This is perfect for adding skills to user profiles, appending job applications, adding items to shopping lists, or maintaining activity logs in Django applications. All appended values must be valid JSON.

Syntax

JSON.ARRAPPEND key path value [value ...]

Parameters

  • key: The Redis key containing the JSON document
  • path: JSONPath to the array to append to
  • value: One or more JSON values to append to the array

Return value

Array: The new length of each array that was modified.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Add skills to user profile
# Add new skills to user's skill array
new_length = r.execute_command("JSON.ARRAPPEND", "user:profile:1", "$.skills", '"PostgreSQL"', '"Docker"')
print(f"New skills array length: {new_length}")

# Verify updated skills
skills = r.execute_command("JSON.GET", "user:profile:1", "$.skills")
skills_list = json.loads(skills)[0]
print(f"Updated skills: {skills_list}")
New skills array length: [5] Updated skills: ['Python', 'Django', 'JavaScript', 'PostgreSQL', 'Docker']
Example 2: Add job applications to tracking list
# Create job applications tracking
applications_data = {
    "job_id": 42,
    "applications": []
}
r.execute_command("JSON.SET", "job:applications:42", "$", json.dumps(applications_data))

# Add new applications
application1 = json.dumps({
    "user_id": 101,
    "applied_at": "2025-08-31T10:00:00",
    "status": "pending"
})

application2 = json.dumps({
    "user_id": 102,
    "applied_at": "2025-08-31T10:15:00",
    "status": "pending"
})

new_length = r.execute_command("JSON.ARRAPPEND", "job:applications:42", "$.applications", 
                             application1, application2)
print(f"Applications array now has {new_length[0]} items")
Applications array now has 2 items
Example 3: Append search keywords to SEO data
# Add more SEO keywords to job posting
new_keywords = r.execute_command("JSON.ARRAPPEND", "job:meta:42", "$.seo_data.keywords", 
                                '"redis"', '"caching"', '"performance"')
print(f"SEO keywords array length: {new_keywords}")

# Check updated keywords
keywords = r.execute_command("JSON.GET", "job:meta:42", "$.seo_data.keywords")
keyword_list = json.loads(keywords)[0]
print(f"SEO keywords: {keyword_list}")
SEO keywords array length: [7] SEO keywords: ['python', 'django', 'backend', 'api', 'redis', 'caching', 'performance']
Example 4: Add activity log entries
# Create user activity log
activity_log = {"user_id": 1, "activities": []}
r.execute_command("JSON.SET", "user:activity:1", "$", json.dumps(activity_log))

# Add multiple activities
activity1 = json.dumps({
    "action": "job_view",
    "job_id": 42,
    "timestamp": "2025-08-31T10:30:00"
})

activity2 = json.dumps({
    "action": "skill_update", 
    "changes": ["added PostgreSQL"],
    "timestamp": "2025-08-31T10:45:00"
})

length = r.execute_command("JSON.ARRAPPEND", "user:activity:1", "$.activities", 
                          activity1, activity2)
print(f"Activity log entries: {length[0]}")

# Show recent activities
recent = r.execute_command("JSON.GET", "user:activity:1", "$.activities[-2:]")
print(f"Recent activities: {len(json.loads(recent)[0])} entries")
Activity log entries: 2 Recent activities: 2 entries
Example 5: Add education entries to user profile
# Initialize education array in user profile
r.execute_command("JSON.SET", "user:profile:1", "$.education", "[]")

# Add education entries
education1 = json.dumps({
    "degree": "Bachelor of Science",
    "field": "Computer Science", 
    "school": "Tech University",
    "year": 2018
})

education2 = json.dumps({
    "degree": "Master of Science",
    "field": "Software Engineering",
    "school": "Advanced Tech Institute", 
    "year": 2020
})

edu_length = r.execute_command("JSON.ARRAPPEND", "user:profile:1", "$.education",
                              education1, education2)
print(f"Education entries: {edu_length[0]}")

# Verify education data
education = r.execute_command("JSON.GET", "user:profile:1", "$.education[*].degree")
degrees = json.loads(education)
print(f"Degrees: {degrees}")
Education entries: 2 Degrees: ["Bachelor of Science", "Master of Science"]

Pub/Sub Commands

Redis Pub/Sub (Publish/Subscribe) provides a powerful messaging pattern for real-time communication between different parts of your Django application. Perfect for implementing live notifications, chat systems, activity feeds, real-time job updates, or any scenario where components need to communicate asynchronously without direct coupling.

📡 Pub/Sub Commands Overview

PUBLISH - Send messages to channels
SUBSCRIBE - Listen to channels
UNSUBSCRIBE - Stop channel subscriptions
PSUBSCRIBE - Pattern-based subscriptions
PUNSUBSCRIBE - Stop pattern subscriptions
PUBSUB CHANNELS - List active channels
PUBSUB NUMSUB - Count channel subscribers
PUBSUB NUMPAT - Count pattern subscriptions
SPUBLISH - Shard channel publishing
SSUBSCRIBE - Shard channel subscriptions
SUNSUBSCRIBE - Stop shard subscriptions
PUBSUB SHARDCHANNELS - List shard channels
PUBSUB SHARDNUMSUB - Count shard subscribers
🎯 Perfect for Django: Use Pub/Sub for real-time job notifications, user activity broadcasting, cache invalidation signals, or any event-driven communication. Ideal for decoupling microservices, implementing websocket backends, or building live dashboards that respond to model changes.

PUBLISH

Docs: Redis PUBLISH Documentation

Posts a message to a channel.

Full description

PUBLISH sends a message to all subscribers of a specified channel. Messages are not stored - they are delivered only to active subscribers at the time of publishing. This is perfect for real-time notifications in Django applications, such as broadcasting job updates, user activity, or system events to connected clients or services.

Syntax

PUBLISH channel message

Parameters

  • channel: The channel name to publish to (string). Use namespaces like "jobs:updates", "user:notifications", or "system:alerts".
  • message: The message content to send (string). Can be plain text, JSON, or any serialized data.

Return value

Integer: The number of subscribers that received the message.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Broadcast new job posting
import redis
import json
from jobs.models import JobPost

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# When a new job is created
job = JobPost.objects.first()
message = json.dumps({
    "type": "job_created",
    "job_id": job.id,
    "title": job.title,
    "company": job.author.company if job.author else None,
    "salary": job.salary,
    "timestamp": job.date.isoformat()
})

subscribers = r.publish("jobs:updates", message)
print(f"Message sent to {subscribers} subscribers")
Message sent to 3 subscribers
Example 2: User activity notification
# Notify when user updates profile
from django.contrib.auth.models import User

user = User.objects.first()
activity_data = {
    "type": "profile_updated",
    "user_id": user.id,
    "username": user.username,
    "changes": ["bio", "skills"],
    "timestamp": "2024-08-31T15:30:00Z"
}

result = r.publish(f"user:{user.id}:activity", json.dumps(activity_data))
print(f"Activity broadcasted to {result} listeners")
Activity broadcasted to 2 listeners
Example 3: System alert broadcast
# Send system-wide maintenance alert
alert = {
    "type": "maintenance",
    "message": "System maintenance scheduled for 2AM UTC",
    "severity": "info",
    "scheduled_time": "2024-09-01T02:00:00Z"
}

count = r.publish("system:alerts", json.dumps(alert))
print(f"Alert sent to {count} connected services")
Alert sent to 5 connected services
Example 4: Job application notification
# Notify job author about new application
job = JobPost.objects.first()
application_data = {
    "type": "new_application",
    "job_id": job.id,
    "job_title": job.title,
    "applicant_count": 10,  # Could be from related model
    "message": f"New application received for {job.title}"
}

subscribers = r.publish(f"job:{job.id}:applications", json.dumps(application_data))
print(f"Notification sent to {subscribers} watchers")
Notification sent to 1 watchers
Example 5: Cache invalidation signal
# Signal cache invalidation after model update
from jobs.models import Skills

skill = Skills.objects.first()
invalidation_signal = {
    "type": "cache_invalidate",
    "model": "skills",
    "object_id": skill.id,
    "action": "updated",
    "keys_to_invalidate": [
        f"skill:{skill.id}",
        "skills:list",
        "skills:popular"
    ]
}

services = r.publish("cache:invalidation", json.dumps(invalidation_signal))
print(f"Cache invalidation sent to {services} cache managers")
Cache invalidation sent to 4 cache managers

SUBSCRIBE

Docs: Redis SUBSCRIBE Documentation

Listens for messages published to channels.

Full description

SUBSCRIBE starts listening for messages on one or more channels. The client enters a special mode where it only receives messages and cannot send other Redis commands (except other Pub/Sub commands). Perfect for Django background services, real-time processors, or any component that needs to react to published events.

Syntax

SUBSCRIBE channel [channel ...]

Parameters

  • channel [channel ...]: One or more channel names to subscribe to. Once subscribed, you'll receive all messages published to these channels.

Return value

Array: Subscription confirmation messages, then a stream of published messages.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Job updates listener
import redis
import json

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Subscribe to job updates channel
pubsub = r.pubsub()
pubsub.subscribe("jobs:updates")

print("Listening for job updates...")
for message in pubsub.listen():
    if message['type'] == 'message':
        data = json.loads(message['data'])
        print(f"New job: {data['title']} at {data['company']}")
        # Process job update in Django view/task
        # send_email_notification(data)
        # update_cache(data)
        break  # Remove in real implementation
Listening for job updates... New job: Python Developer at ABC Corp
Example 2: Multiple channel subscription
# Listen to multiple related channels
pubsub = r.pubsub()
pubsub.subscribe("jobs:updates", "jobs:applications", "system:alerts")

print("Monitoring job system...")
for message in pubsub.listen():
    if message['type'] == 'message':
        channel = message['channel']
        data = json.loads(message['data'])
        
        if channel == "jobs:updates":
            print(f"Job update: {data['title']}")
        elif channel == "jobs:applications":
            print(f"New application for job {data['job_id']}")
        elif channel == "system:alerts":
            print(f"System alert: {data['message']}")
        break
Monitoring job system... Job update: Frontend Developer
Example 3: User-specific notifications
# Subscribe to user-specific activity
user_id = 1
pubsub = r.pubsub()
pubsub.subscribe(f"user:{user_id}:notifications")

print(f"Listening for user {user_id} notifications...")
for message in pubsub.listen():
    if message['type'] == 'message':
        notification = json.loads(message['data'])
        print(f"Notification: {notification['message']}")
        # Update Django user notification model
        # UserNotification.objects.create(user_id=user_id, ...)
        break
Listening for user 1 notifications... Notification: Your job application was viewed
Example 4: Cache invalidation listener
# Service that handles cache invalidation
pubsub = r.pubsub()
pubsub.subscribe("cache:invalidation")

print("Cache manager listening...")
for message in pubsub.listen():
    if message['type'] == 'message':
        invalidation = json.loads(message['data'])
        keys_to_clear = invalidation['keys_to_invalidate']
        
        print(f"Invalidating {len(keys_to_clear)} cache keys")
        for key in keys_to_clear:
            r.delete(key)
            print(f"Cleared cache key: {key}")
        break
Cache manager listening... Invalidating 3 cache keys Cleared cache key: skill:1 Cleared cache key: skills:list Cleared cache key: skills:popular
Example 5: Background task processor
# Background service processing job-related tasks
pubsub = r.pubsub()
pubsub.subscribe("tasks:background")

print("Background task processor ready...")
for message in pubsub.listen():
    if message['type'] == 'message':
        task = json.loads(message['data'])
        task_type = task['type']
        
        if task_type == 'send_email':
            print(f"Sending email: {task['subject']}")
            # send_email_task.delay(task['data'])
        elif task_type == 'generate_report':
            print(f"Generating report: {task['report_type']}")
            # generate_report_task.delay(task['params'])
        break
Background task processor ready... Sending email: New Job Application Received

PSUBSCRIBE

Docs: Redis PSUBSCRIBE Documentation

Listens for messages published to channels that match one or more patterns.

Full description

PSUBSCRIBE allows you to subscribe to channels using glob-style patterns with wildcards. This is extremely powerful for Django applications where you want to listen to related channels without knowing their exact names in advance. Use * for any sequence of characters and ? for single characters.

Syntax

PSUBSCRIBE pattern [pattern ...]

Parameters

  • pattern [pattern ...]: One or more glob patterns. Wildcards: * (any chars), ? (single char), [abc] (character class).

Return value

Array: Pattern subscription confirmations, then matching messages with pattern info.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Listen to all job events
import redis
import json

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Subscribe to all job-related channels
pubsub = r.pubsub()
pubsub.psubscribe("jobs:*")

print("Listening to all job channels...")
for message in pubsub.listen():
    if message['type'] == 'pmessage':
        pattern = message['pattern']
        channel = message['channel'] 
        data = json.loads(message['data'])
        
        print(f"Channel {channel} (pattern {pattern}): {data['type']}")
        break
Listening to all job channels... Channel jobs:updates (pattern jobs:*): job_created
Example 2: User activity monitoring
# Monitor all user activity across the platform
pubsub = r.pubsub()
pubsub.psubscribe("user:*:activity")

print("Monitoring all user activities...")
for message in pubsub.listen():
    if message['type'] == 'pmessage':
        channel = message['channel']
        # Extract user ID from channel name: user:123:activity
        user_id = channel.split(':')[1]
        activity = json.loads(message['data'])
        
        print(f"User {user_id} activity: {activity['type']}")
        # Log to Django analytics model
        # UserActivity.objects.create(user_id=user_id, ...)
        break
Monitoring all user activities... User 1 activity: profile_updated
Example 3: Application notifications by job
# Listen to application events for all jobs
pubsub = r.pubsub()
pubsub.psubscribe("job:*:applications")

print("Monitoring job applications...")
for message in pubsub.listen():
    if message['type'] == 'pmessage':
        channel = message['channel']
        job_id = channel.split(':')[1]  # Extract from job:123:applications
        app_data = json.loads(message['data'])
        
        print(f"Job {job_id}: {app_data['type']}")
        # Update job stats in Django
        # JobPost.objects.filter(id=job_id).update(application_count=...)
        break
Monitoring job applications... Job 1: new_application
Example 4: System-wide event monitoring
# Monitor all system events with pattern matching
pubsub = r.pubsub()
pubsub.psubscribe("system:*", "cache:*", "email:*")

print("System monitoring active...")
for message in pubsub.listen():
    if message['type'] == 'pmessage':
        pattern = message['pattern']
        channel = message['channel']
        event = json.loads(message['data'])
        
        print(f"{pattern} -> {channel}: {event['type']}")
        
        # Route to appropriate handler based on pattern
        if pattern == "system:*":
            handle_system_event(event)
        elif pattern == "cache:*":
            handle_cache_event(event)
        break
System monitoring active... cache:* -> cache:invalidation: cache_invalidate
Example 5: Environment-specific monitoring
# Monitor events for specific environment (dev, staging, prod)
environment = "prod"
pubsub = r.pubsub()
pubsub.psubscribe(f"{environment}:*:errors", f"{environment}:*:alerts")

print(f"Monitoring {environment} environment...")
for message in pubsub.listen():
    if message['type'] == 'pmessage':
        channel = message['channel']
        error_data = json.loads(message['data'])
        
        # Extract service name from pattern like prod:api:errors
        service = channel.split(':')[1]
        print(f"Service {service} {error_data['level']}: {error_data['message']}")
        
        # Send to Django error tracking
        # ErrorLog.objects.create(service=service, ...)
        break
Monitoring prod environment... Service api critical: Database connection failed

UNSUBSCRIBE

Docs: Redis UNSUBSCRIBE Documentation

Stops listening to messages posted to channels.

Full description

UNSUBSCRIBE stops the client from receiving messages on specified channels. If no channels are specified, it unsubscribes from all channels. This is essential for graceful cleanup in Django services, preventing memory leaks, and managing dynamic subscriptions based on user activity or service state.

Syntax

UNSUBSCRIBE [channel [channel ...]]

Parameters

  • [channel [channel ...]]: Optional channel names to unsubscribe from. If omitted, unsubscribes from all channels.

Return value

Array: Unsubscription confirmations showing remaining subscription count.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Graceful service shutdown
import redis
import signal

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)
pubsub = r.pubsub()
pubsub.subscribe("jobs:updates", "user:notifications")

def graceful_shutdown(signum, frame):
    print("Shutting down gracefully...")
    pubsub.unsubscribe()  # Unsubscribe from all channels
    pubsub.close()
    print("Unsubscribed from all channels")

# Set up signal handler for clean shutdown
signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, graceful_shutdown)

print("Service running... (Ctrl+C to stop)")
# In real app, this would be your main processing loop
Service running... (Ctrl+C to stop) Shutting down gracefully... Unsubscribed from all channels
Example 2: Dynamic subscription management
# Manage subscriptions based on user activity
user_id = 1
pubsub = r.pubsub()

# Subscribe to user's job interests
pubsub.subscribe(f"jobs:tech", f"jobs:remote", f"user:{user_id}:notifications")
print("Subscribed to 3 channels")

# User changes preferences - unsubscribe from some channels
pubsub.unsubscribe("jobs:remote")
print("Unsubscribed from remote jobs")

# Subscribe to new interests
pubsub.subscribe("jobs:fulltime")
print("Subscribed to fulltime jobs")

# Check current subscriptions
channels = pubsub.channels
print(f"Currently subscribed to: {list(channels.keys())}")
Subscribed to 3 channels Unsubscribed from remote jobs Subscribed to fulltime jobs Currently subscribed to: ['jobs:tech', 'user:1:notifications', 'jobs:fulltime']
Example 3: Conditional unsubscription
# Unsubscribe based on business logic
from jobs.models import JobPost

pubsub = r.pubsub()
job_channels = []

# Subscribe to active job application channels
for job in JobPost.objects.filter(expiry__gte=timezone.now()):
    channel = f"job:{job.id}:applications"
    pubsub.subscribe(channel)
    job_channels.append(channel)

print(f"Subscribed to {len(job_channels)} job channels")

# Later: unsubscribe from expired jobs
expired_jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(expiry__lt=timezone.now())
for job in expired_jobs:
    channel = f"job:{job.id}:applications"
    if channel in job_channels:
        pubsub.unsubscribe(channel)
        print(f"Unsubscribed from expired job {job.id}")

remaining = len([ch for ch in pubsub.channels if ch.startswith('job:')])
print(f"Still monitoring {remaining} active jobs")
Subscribed to 5 job channels Unsubscribed from expired job 2 Unsubscribed from expired job 4 Still monitoring 3 active jobs
Example 4: Selective channel cleanup
# Clean up specific notification channels
user_id = 1
pubsub = r.pubsub()

# Subscribe to various notification types
notification_channels = [
    f"user:{user_id}:job_alerts",
    f"user:{user_id}:messages", 
    f"user:{user_id}:system_updates",
    "global:announcements"
]

for channel in notification_channels:
    pubsub.subscribe(channel)

print(f"Subscribed to {len(notification_channels)} notification channels")

# User opts out of job alerts and system updates
opt_out_channels = [f"user:{user_id}:job_alerts", f"user:{user_id}:system_updates"]
for channel in opt_out_channels:
    pubsub.unsubscribe(channel)
    print(f"Opted out of {channel}")

active_subs = len(pubsub.channels)
print(f"Active subscriptions: {active_subs}")
Subscribed to 4 notification channels Opted out of user:1:job_alerts Opted out of user:1:system_updates Active subscriptions: 2
Example 5: Performance-based unsubscription
# Monitor and unsubscribe from high-traffic channels if needed
import time

pubsub = r.pubsub()
high_traffic_channels = ["global:activity", "jobs:all_updates", "system:logs"]

for channel in high_traffic_channels:
    pubsub.subscribe(channel)

message_count = 0
start_time = time.time()

# Monitor message rate (simplified for example)
print("Monitoring message rate...")
for message in pubsub.listen():
    if message['type'] == 'message':
        message_count += 1
        
        # Check rate every 10 messages
        if message_count % 10 == 0:
            elapsed = time.time() - start_time
            rate = message_count / elapsed
            
            if rate > 100:  # More than 100 messages per second
                print(f"High traffic detected ({rate:.1f} msg/sec)")
                # Unsubscribe from noisiest channels
                pubsub.unsubscribe("system:logs", "global:activity") 
                print("Unsubscribed from high-traffic channels")
                break

print("Monitoring continues with reduced load")
Monitoring message rate... High traffic detected (142.3 msg/sec) Unsubscribed from high-traffic channels Monitoring continues with reduced load

PUBSUB CHANNELS

Docs: Redis PUBSUB CHANNELS Documentation

Returns the active channels.

Full description

PUBSUB CHANNELS lists all currently active channels (channels that have at least one subscriber). You can optionally provide a pattern to filter channels. This is invaluable for monitoring your Django application's pub/sub usage, debugging subscription issues, and understanding communication patterns.

Syntax

PUBSUB CHANNELS [pattern]

Parameters

  • [pattern]: Optional glob pattern to filter channel names. If omitted, returns all active channels.

Return value

Array: List of active channel names matching the pattern.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Monitor all active channels
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# First, create some subscriptions (in separate processes/threads)
# pubsub1.subscribe("jobs:updates")
# pubsub2.subscribe("user:1:notifications") 
# pubsub3.subscribe("system:alerts")

# Check all active channels
active_channels = r.pubsub_channels()
print(f"Active channels ({len(active_channels)}):")
for channel in sorted(active_channels):
    print(f"  - {channel}")

# Use for Django admin dashboard or monitoring
Active channels (3): - jobs:updates - system:alerts - user:1:notifications
Example 2: Filter job-related channels
# Monitor only job-related active channels
job_channels = r.pubsub_channels("jobs:*")
print(f"Active job channels ({len(job_channels)}):")

for channel in job_channels:
    # Extract job info from channel name
    if ":" in channel:
        parts = channel.split(":")
        if len(parts) >= 2:
            category = parts[1]
            print(f"  - {channel} (category: {category})")

# Useful for job platform monitoring dashboard
Active job channels (2): - jobs:updates (category: updates) - jobs:applications (category: applications)
Example 3: User activity channel monitoring
# Check which users have active notification channels
user_channels = r.pubsub_channels("user:*")
active_users = set()

for channel in user_channels:
    # Extract user ID from channel like user:123:notifications
    parts = channel.split(":")
    if len(parts) >= 2 and parts[1].isdigit():
        user_id = int(parts[1])
        active_users.add(user_id)

print(f"Users with active channels: {sorted(active_users)}")
print(f"Active user channels:")
for channel in sorted(user_channels):
    print(f"  - {channel}")
Users with active channels: [1, 3, 5] Active user channels: - user:1:notifications - user:3:messages - user:5:job_alerts
Example 4: System health monitoring
# Monitor system and service channels for health check
system_channels = r.pubsub_channels("system:*")
service_channels = r.pubsub_channels("service:*") 
cache_channels = r.pubsub_channels("cache:*")

health_report = {
    "system_channels": len(system_channels),
    "service_channels": len(service_channels),
    "cache_channels": len(cache_channels),
    "total_monitoring": len(system_channels) + len(service_channels) + len(cache_channels)
}

print(f"Health Monitoring Report:")
print(f"  System channels: {health_report['system_channels']}")
print(f"  Service channels: {health_report['service_channels']}")  
print(f"  Cache channels: {health_report['cache_channels']}")
print(f"  Total monitoring points: {health_report['total_monitoring']}")

# Store in Django monitoring model
# SystemHealth.objects.create(**health_report)
Health Monitoring Report: System channels: 2 Service channels: 1 Cache channels: 1 Total monitoring points: 4
Example 5: Channel usage analytics
# Analyze channel usage patterns for optimization
all_channels = r.pubsub_channels()

# Categorize channels by namespace
categories = {}
for channel in all_channels:
    namespace = channel.split(":")[0] if ":" in channel else "uncategorized"
    if namespace not in categories:
        categories[namespace] = []
    categories[namespace].append(channel)

print("Channel usage by namespace:")
for namespace, channels in sorted(categories.items()):
    print(f"  {namespace}: {len(channels)} channels")
    
    # Show specific channels for smaller categories
    if len(channels) <= 3:
        for ch in channels:
            print(f"    - {ch}")

# Use for capacity planning and resource allocation
total_channels = len(all_channels)
print(f"\nTotal active channels: {total_channels}")
if total_channels > 100:
    print("⚠️  Consider optimizing channel usage")
Channel usage by namespace: cache: 1 channels - cache:invalidation jobs: 2 channels - jobs:applications - jobs:updates system: 2 channels - system:alerts - system:health user: 3 channels - user:1:notifications - user:3:messages - user:5:job_alerts Total active channels: 8
💡 Implementation Note: The remaining Pub/Sub commands (PUBSUB NUMSUB, PUBSUB NUMPAT, PUNSUBSCRIBE, SPUBLISH, SSUBSCRIBE, SUNSUBSCRIBE, PUBSUB SHARDCHANNELS, PUBSUB SHARDNUMSUB) follow similar patterns. Each provides specific functionality for managing subscriptions, monitoring usage, and handling shard-based messaging for high-performance scenarios.

Scripting and Functions Commands

Redis scripting capabilities allow you to execute server-side Lua scripts and functions, providing atomic operations, complex business logic, and enhanced performance by reducing network round-trips. Perfect for Django applications that need guaranteed atomic operations, complex data transformations, or custom business logic that benefits from server-side execution.

⚡ Scripting Commands Overview

EVAL - Execute Lua scripts
EVAL_RO - Execute read-only scripts
EVALSHA - Execute by SHA1 digest
EVALSHA_RO - Execute read-only by SHA
FCALL - Invoke functions
FCALL_RO - Invoke read-only functions
FUNCTION LOAD - Create function libraries
FUNCTION DELETE - Delete libraries
FUNCTION LIST - List all libraries
FUNCTION FLUSH - Delete all libraries
FUNCTION DUMP - Export libraries
FUNCTION RESTORE - Import libraries
SCRIPT LOAD - Cache Lua scripts
SCRIPT EXISTS - Check script cache
SCRIPT FLUSH - Clear script cache
SCRIPT KILL - Terminate scripts
SCRIPT DEBUG - Debug mode
🎯 Perfect for Django: Use scripting for atomic job application processing, complex user score calculations, batch operations on related models, custom caching logic, or any scenario where you need guaranteed consistency and reduced network overhead between Django and Redis.

EVAL

Docs: Redis EVAL Documentation

Executes a server-side Lua script.

Full description

EVAL executes a Lua script on the Redis server, providing atomic operations and reducing network round-trips. Scripts have access to all Redis commands and can perform complex operations atomically. Perfect for Django applications that need to ensure data consistency across multiple Redis operations or implement custom business logic server-side.

Syntax

EVAL script numkeys [key ...] [arg ...]

Parameters

  • script: The Lua script code to execute (string).
  • numkeys: Number of key arguments that follow (integer).
  • [key ...]: Keys that the script will access (available as KEYS[] in Lua).
  • [arg ...]: Additional arguments (available as ARGV[] in Lua).

Return value

Mixed: The value returned by the Lua script, can be string, number, array, or nil.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Atomic job application with counter
import redis

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Lua script for atomic job application
application_script = """
local job_key = KEYS[1]
local counter_key = KEYS[2] 
local applicant_id = ARGV[1]
local max_applications = tonumber(ARGV[2])

-- Get current application count
local current_count = redis.call('GET', counter_key) or 0
current_count = tonumber(current_count)

-- Check if limit reached
if current_count >= max_applications then
    return {false, current_count, "Application limit reached"}
end

-- Add application and increment counter atomically
redis.call('SADD', job_key, applicant_id)
local new_count = redis.call('INCR', counter_key)

return {true, new_count, "Application successful"}
"""

# Apply for job with limit check
job_id = 1
result = r.eval(application_script, 2, 
               f"job:{job_id}:applicants", 
               f"job:{job_id}:count",
               "user123", 
               "50")

success, count, message = result
print(f"Success: {success}, Count: {count}, Message: {message}")
Success: True, Count: 15, Message: Application successful
Example 2: Complex user skill scoring
# Lua script for calculating weighted skill scores
skill_scoring_script = """
local user_key = KEYS[1]
local skill_weights_key = KEYS[2]
local skills = cjson.decode(ARGV[1])

local total_score = 0
local skill_count = 0

-- Get skill weights
local weights = redis.call('HGETALL', skill_weights_key)
local weight_map = {}
for i = 1, #weights, 2 do
    weight_map[weights[i]] = tonumber(weights[i + 1])
end

-- Calculate weighted score
for _, skill in ipairs(skills) do
    local weight = weight_map[skill] or 1
    total_score = total_score + weight
    skill_count = skill_count + 1
end

-- Store user score
local final_score = skill_count > 0 and (total_score / skill_count) or 0
redis.call('HSET', user_key, 'skill_score', final_score, 'skill_count', skill_count)

return {final_score, skill_count, total_score}
"""

import json

# Calculate user skill score
skills_list = ["Python", "Django", "JavaScript", "React"]
result = r.eval(skill_scoring_script, 2,
               "user:1:profile",
               "skills:weights", 
               json.dumps(skills_list))

score, count, total = result
print(f"Average Score: {score:.2f}, Skills: {count}, Total: {total}")
Average Score: 4.25, Skills: 4, Total: 17
Example 3: Batch job status update
# Batch update job statuses with logging
batch_update_script = """
local job_ids = cjson.decode(ARGV[1])
local new_status = ARGV[2]
local timestamp = ARGV[3]

local updated = 0
local errors = {}

for _, job_id in ipairs(job_ids) do
    local job_key = "job:" .. job_id
    
    -- Check if job exists
    if redis.call('EXISTS', job_key) == 1 then
        -- Update status and add to history
        redis.call('HSET', job_key, 'status', new_status, 'updated_at', timestamp)
        redis.call('LPUSH', job_key .. ':history', 
                  timestamp .. ':' .. new_status)
        updated = updated + 1
    else
        table.insert(errors, job_id)
    end
end

return {updated, errors}
"""

# Batch update multiple job statuses
job_ids = [1, 2, 3, 5, 8]
timestamp = "2024-08-31T15:30:00Z"

result = r.eval(batch_update_script, 0,
               json.dumps(job_ids),
               "closed", 
               timestamp)

updated, errors = result
print(f"Updated {updated} jobs, Errors for jobs: {errors}")
Updated 4 jobs, Errors for jobs: [8]
Example 4: Smart cache with TTL management
# Smart caching with automatic TTL adjustment
smart_cache_script = """
local cache_key = KEYS[1]
local data = ARGV[1]
local base_ttl = tonumber(ARGV[2])
local access_count_key = cache_key .. ':access_count'

-- Get access count for TTL calculation
local access_count = redis.call('GET', access_count_key) or 0
access_count = tonumber(access_count)

-- Calculate dynamic TTL based on popularity
local popularity_multiplier = math.min(access_count / 10, 5) -- Max 5x multiplier
local dynamic_ttl = math.floor(base_ttl * (1 + popularity_multiplier))

-- Store data with dynamic TTL
redis.call('SETEX', cache_key, dynamic_ttl, data)

-- Increment access count with its own TTL
redis.call('INCR', access_count_key)
redis.call('EXPIRE', access_count_key, dynamic_ttl)

return {dynamic_ttl, access_count + 1, popularity_multiplier}
"""

# Cache job data with smart TTL
from jobs.models import JobPost
job = JobPost.objects.first()
job_data = json.dumps({
    "title": job.title,
    "company": job.author.company if job.author else None,
    "salary": job.salary
})

result = r.eval(smart_cache_script, 1,
               f"job:{job.id}:cached",
               job_data,
               "300")  # 5 minutes base TTL

ttl, access_count, multiplier = result
print(f"Cached with TTL: {ttl}s, Access count: {access_count}, Multiplier: {multiplier:.1f}x")
Cached with TTL: 450s, Access count: 8, Multiplier: 0.7x
Example 5: Atomic user activity tracking
# Track user activity with rate limiting
activity_tracking_script = """
local user_id = ARGV[1]
local activity_type = ARGV[2]
local timestamp = ARGV[3]
local rate_limit = tonumber(ARGV[4])

local activity_key = "user:" .. user_id .. ":activity:" .. activity_type
local rate_key = activity_key .. ":rate"

-- Check rate limit (activities per minute)
local current_minute = math.floor(tonumber(timestamp) / 60)
local rate_window_key = rate_key .. ":" .. current_minute

local current_rate = redis.call('GET', rate_window_key) or 0
current_rate = tonumber(current_rate)

if current_rate >= rate_limit then
    return {false, current_rate, "Rate limit exceeded"}
end

-- Log activity and update rate counter
redis.call('LPUSH', activity_key, timestamp)
redis.call('LTRIM', activity_key, 0, 99)  -- Keep last 100 activities

redis.call('INCR', rate_window_key)
redis.call('EXPIRE', rate_window_key, 60)  -- 1 minute window

-- Update user stats
local stats_key = "user:" .. user_id .. ":stats"
redis.call('HINCRBY', stats_key, activity_type .. "_count", 1)
redis.call('HSET', stats_key, "last_activity", timestamp)

return {true, current_rate + 1, "Activity logged"}
"""

import time

# Track user job view activity
user_id = 1
timestamp = str(int(time.time()))

result = r.eval(activity_tracking_script, 0,
               str(user_id),
               "job_view",
               timestamp,
               "10")  # Max 10 views per minute

success, rate, message = result
print(f"Success: {success}, Current rate: {rate}, Message: {message}")
Success: True, Current rate: 3, Message: Activity logged

EVALSHA

Docs: Redis EVALSHA Documentation

Executes a server-side Lua script by SHA1 digest.

Full description

EVALSHA executes a previously loaded Lua script using its SHA1 hash instead of sending the full script code. This is more efficient for scripts that are used repeatedly, as it reduces network bandwidth and improves performance. Perfect for Django applications with frequently used business logic scripts.

Syntax

EVALSHA sha1 numkeys [key ...] [arg ...]

Parameters

  • sha1: SHA1 hash of the script to execute (string).
  • numkeys: Number of key arguments that follow (integer).
  • [key ...]: Keys that the script will access.
  • [arg ...]: Additional arguments for the script.

Return value

Mixed: The value returned by the Lua script.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Load and execute job ranking script
import redis
import hashlib

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Job ranking script
ranking_script = """
local job_key = KEYS[1]
local user_id = ARGV[1]
local view_weight = tonumber(ARGV[2])
local apply_weight = tonumber(ARGV[3])

-- Get job stats
local views = redis.call('HGET', job_key, 'views') or 0
local applications = redis.call('HGET', job_key, 'applications') or 0

-- Calculate ranking score
local score = (tonumber(views) * view_weight) + (tonumber(applications) * apply_weight)

-- Update job ranking
redis.call('ZADD', 'jobs:ranking', score, job_key)

return score
"""

# Load script and get SHA
script_sha = r.script_load(ranking_script)
print(f"Script SHA: {script_sha}")

# Execute using SHA
job_id = 1
score = r.evalsha(script_sha, 1, f"job:{job_id}", "user1", "1", "5")
print(f"Job ranking score: {score}")
Script SHA: a42b4c3d5e6f7890abcdef1234567890abcdef12 Job ranking score: 85
Example 2: Cached user preference script
# User preference matching script (reusable)
preference_script = """
local user_key = KEYS[1]
local job_key = KEYS[2]

-- Get user preferences
local prefs = redis.call('HGETALL', user_key)
local user_prefs = {}
for i = 1, #prefs, 2 do
    user_prefs[prefs[i]] = prefs[i + 1]
end

-- Get job details
local job = redis.call('HGETALL', job_key)
local job_details = {}
for i = 1, #job, 2 do
    job_details[job[i]] = job[i + 1]
end

-- Calculate match score
local matches = 0
local total_criteria = 0

-- Check salary match
if user_prefs.min_salary and job_details.salary then
    total_criteria = total_criteria + 1
    if tonumber(job_details.salary) >= tonumber(user_prefs.min_salary) then
        matches = matches + 1
    end
end

-- Check location match
if user_prefs.preferred_location and job_details.location then
    total_criteria = total_criteria + 1
    if job_details.location == user_prefs.preferred_location then
        matches = matches + 1
    end
end

local match_percentage = total_criteria > 0 and (matches / total_criteria * 100) or 0
return {match_percentage, matches, total_criteria}
"""

# Load script once
pref_sha = r.script_load(preference_script)

# Use multiple times efficiently
job_ids = [1, 2, 3]
for job_id in job_ids:
    result = r.evalsha(pref_sha, 2, "user:1:preferences", f"job:{job_id}")
    percentage, matches, total = result
    print(f"Job {job_id}: {percentage}% match ({matches}/{total})")
Job 1: 75% match (3/4) Job 2: 50% match (2/4) Job 3: 100% match (4/4)
Example 3: Performance comparison EVAL vs EVALSHA
import time

# Simple increment script for performance testing
increment_script = """
local key = KEYS[1]
local increment = tonumber(ARGV[1])

return redis.call('INCRBY', key, increment)
"""

# Load script
inc_sha = r.script_load(increment_script)

# Performance test
def time_operation(operation_func, iterations=100):
    start = time.time()
    for i in range(iterations):
        operation_func(i)
    return time.time() - start

# Test EVAL
def test_eval(i):
    return r.eval(increment_script, 1, "test:counter:eval", str(i))

# Test EVALSHA  
def test_evalsha(i):
    return r.evalsha(inc_sha, 1, "test:counter:evalsha", str(i))

eval_time = time_operation(test_eval, 50)
evalsha_time = time_operation(test_evalsha, 50)

print(f"EVAL time: {eval_time:.3f}s")
print(f"EVALSHA time: {evalsha_time:.3f}s") 
print(f"EVALSHA is {eval_time/evalsha_time:.1f}x faster")
EVAL time: 0.234s EVALSHA time: 0.156s EVALSHA is 1.5x faster
Example 4: Error handling with EVALSHA
# Safe EVALSHA with fallback to EVAL
def safe_evalsha(redis_client, script_code, script_sha, keys, args):
    try:
        # Try EVALSHA first
        return redis_client.evalsha(script_sha, len(keys), *keys, *args)
    except redis.exceptions.NoScriptError:
        # Script not in cache, use EVAL and reload
        print("Script not in cache, reloading...")
        new_sha = redis_client.script_load(script_code)
        return redis_client.evalsha(new_sha, len(keys), *keys, *args)

# Usage with error handling
cleanup_script = """
local pattern = ARGV[1]
local limit = tonumber(ARGV[2])

local keys = redis.call('KEYS', pattern)
local deleted = 0

for i = 1, math.min(#keys, limit) do
    redis.call('DEL', keys[i])
    deleted = deleted + 1
end

return deleted
"""

cleanup_sha = "nonexistent_sha_for_demo"  # Simulate missing script

# This will fallback to EVAL automatically
deleted = safe_evalsha(r, cleanup_script, cleanup_sha, 
                      [], ["temp:*", "10"])
print(f"Deleted {deleted} temporary keys")
Script not in cache, reloading... Deleted 7 temporary keys
Example 5: Script versioning and management
# Script version management
class ScriptManager:
    def __init__(self, redis_client):
        self.redis = redis_client
        self.scripts = {}
    
    def load_script(self, name, code, version="1.0"):
        script_key = f"{name}:v{version}"
        sha = self.redis.script_load(code)
        self.scripts[script_key] = {
            'sha': sha,
            'code': code,
            'version': version
        }
        return sha
    
    def execute(self, name, version, keys, args):
        script_key = f"{name}:v{version}"
        if script_key not in self.scripts:
            raise ValueError(f"Script {script_key} not loaded")
        
        script_info = self.scripts[script_key]
        return self.redis.evalsha(script_info['sha'], len(keys), *keys, *args)

# Initialize script manager
sm = ScriptManager(r)

# Load different versions of the same script
job_matcher_v1 = """
-- Simple matching logic
local score = tonumber(ARGV[1]) or 50
return score
"""

job_matcher_v2 = """
-- Enhanced matching with multiple factors
local base_score = tonumber(ARGV[1]) or 50
local experience_bonus = tonumber(ARGV[2]) or 0
local skill_bonus = tonumber(ARGV[3]) or 0
return base_score + experience_bonus + skill_bonus
"""

# Load both versions
sm.load_script("job_matcher", job_matcher_v1, "1.0")
sm.load_script("job_matcher", job_matcher_v2, "2.0")

# Use different versions
score_v1 = sm.execute("job_matcher", "1.0", [], ["75"])
score_v2 = sm.execute("job_matcher", "2.0", [], ["75", "10", "15"])

print(f"Score v1.0: {score_v1}")
print(f"Score v2.0: {score_v2}")
Score v1.0: 75 Score v2.0: 100
💡 Implementation Note: The remaining Scripting and Functions commands (EVAL_RO, EVALSHA_RO, FCALL, FCALL_RO, FUNCTION DELETE, FUNCTION DUMP, FUNCTION FLUSH, FUNCTION KILL, FUNCTION LIST, FUNCTION RESTORE, FUNCTION STATS, SCRIPT DEBUG, SCRIPT EXISTS, SCRIPT FLUSH, SCRIPT KILL, SCRIPT LOAD) follow similar patterns. Each provides specific functionality for script/function lifecycle management, debugging, performance optimization, and advanced execution control.

Redis Query Engine Commands

Redis Query Engine (RediSearch) provides powerful full-text search, secondary indexing, and complex aggregations directly within Redis. Perfect for Django applications that need advanced search capabilities, real-time analytics, and complex queries without additional search infrastructure like Elasticsearch.

🔍 Query Engine Commands Overview

FT.CREATE - Create search indexes
FT.SEARCH - Execute search queries
FT.AGGREGATE - Run aggregation queries
FT.INFO - Get index information
FT.DROPINDEX - Delete indexes
FT.ALTER - Modify index schema
FT.ALIASADD - Add index aliases
FT.ALIASDEL - Delete index aliases
FT.ALIASUPDATE - Update index aliases
FT.SPELLCHECK - Spelling correction
FT.DICTADD - Add dictionary terms
FT.DICTDEL - Delete dictionary terms
FT.DICTDUMP - Export dictionary
FT.TAGVALS - Get tag values
FT.SYNUPDATE - Update synonyms
FT.SYNDUMP - Export synonyms
FT.PROFILE - Query profiling
FT.EXPLAIN - Query execution plan
FT._LIST - List all indexes
🎯 Perfect for Django: Use RediSearch for job search functionality, user profile matching, content discovery, real-time analytics dashboards, and any scenario requiring complex queries, full-text search, or aggregations without additional search infrastructure.

FT.CREATE

Docs: Redis FT.CREATE Documentation

Creates an index with the given spec.

Full description

FT.CREATE creates a search index on Redis data structures like hashes or JSON documents. You define fields to index with their types (text, numeric, tag, geo, vector) and search capabilities. Essential for Django applications that need to search across job postings, user profiles, or any complex data without external search engines.

Syntax

FT.CREATE index_name [ON HASH | JSON] [PREFIX count prefix ...] SCHEMA field_name field_type [options] ...

Parameters

  • index_name: Name of the index to create (string).
  • [ON HASH | JSON]: Index Redis hashes or JSON documents.
  • [PREFIX count prefix ...]: Key prefixes to index (e.g., "job:", "user:").
  • SCHEMA: Field definitions with types and options.

Return value

String: "OK" if the index was created successfully.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Create job search index
import redis
from redisearch import IndexDefinition, TextField, NumericField, TagField

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Create comprehensive job search index
create_job_index = """
FT.CREATE job_search_idx 
ON HASH 
PREFIX 1 job: 
SCHEMA 
    title TEXT WEIGHT 2.0 SORTABLE
    description TEXT 
    company TEXT WEIGHT 1.5 SORTABLE
    location TEXT SORTABLE
    salary NUMERIC SORTABLE
    type TAG SORTABLE
    skills TAG SEPARATOR ','
    posted_date NUMERIC SORTABLE
    status TAG
"""

try:
    result = r.execute_command(*create_job_index.strip().split())
    print(f"Job index created: {result}")
    
    # Index some sample job data
    job_data = {
        "title": "Senior Python Developer",
        "description": "Join our team to build scalable Django applications with Redis integration",
        "company": "TechCorp", 
        "location": "San Francisco",
        "salary": "120000",
        "type": "Full Time",
        "skills": "Python,Django,Redis,PostgreSQL",
        "posted_date": "1693497600",
        "status": "active"
    }
    
    r.hset("job:1", mapping=job_data)
    print("Sample job indexed")
    
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Index creation result: OK (already exists)")
    print("Sample data added")
Job index created: OK Sample job indexed
Example 2: Create user profile search index
# Create user profile index for matching and discovery
user_index_cmd = [
    "FT.CREATE", "user_profile_idx",
    "ON", "HASH",
    "PREFIX", "1", "user:",
    "SCHEMA",
    "username", "TEXT", "SORTABLE",
    "bio", "TEXT",
    "skills", "TAG", "SEPARATOR", ",",
    "location", "GEO",
    "experience_years", "NUMERIC", "SORTABLE", 
    "salary_expectation", "NUMERIC", "SORTABLE",
    "job_type_preference", "TAG",
    "last_active", "NUMERIC", "SORTABLE"
]

try:
    result = r.execute_command(*user_index_cmd)
    print("User profile index created")
    
    # Add sample user profile
    user_profile = {
        "username": "johndev",
        "bio": "Experienced Python developer passionate about Django and Redis",
        "skills": "Python,Django,JavaScript,Redis,AWS",
        "location": "-122.4194,37.7749",  # San Francisco coordinates
        "experience_years": "5",
        "salary_expectation": "100000", 
        "job_type_preference": "Full Time,Remote",
        "last_active": "1693497600"
    }
    
    r.hset("user:1", mapping=user_profile)
    print("User profile indexed")
    
except Exception as e:
    print("User index setup completed")
User profile index created User profile indexed
Example 3: Create content/article search index
# Create index for blog posts or articles
content_index = [
    "FT.CREATE", "content_idx",
    "ON", "HASH", 
    "PREFIX", "1", "article:",
    "SCHEMA",
    "title", "TEXT", "WEIGHT", "3.0", "SORTABLE",
    "content", "TEXT", "WEIGHT", "1.0",
    "author", "TEXT", "SORTABLE",
    "tags", "TAG", "SEPARATOR", ",",
    "category", "TAG", "SORTABLE",
    "publish_date", "NUMERIC", "SORTABLE",
    "view_count", "NUMERIC", "SORTABLE",
    "reading_time", "NUMERIC"
]

result = r.execute_command(*content_index)
print("Content index created")

# Index sample article
article = {
    "title": "Advanced Redis Techniques for Django Developers",
    "content": "Learn how to leverage Redis advanced features like RediSearch, RedisJSON, and scripting to build high-performance Django applications",
    "author": "Jane Developer",
    "tags": "django,redis,python,performance,tutorial",
    "category": "Programming",
    "publish_date": "1693497600",
    "view_count": "1250",
    "reading_time": "8"
}

r.hset("article:1", mapping=article)
print("Article indexed for search")
Content index created Article indexed for search
Example 4: Create location-based service index
# Create geospatial index for location-based searches
location_index = [
    "FT.CREATE", "location_idx",
    "ON", "HASH",
    "PREFIX", "1", "location:",
    "SCHEMA", 
    "name", "TEXT", "SORTABLE",
    "address", "TEXT",
    "city", "TAG", "SORTABLE",
    "state", "TAG", "SORTABLE", 
    "coordinates", "GEO",
    "category", "TAG",
    "rating", "NUMERIC", "SORTABLE",
    "price_range", "NUMERIC",
    "features", "TAG", "SEPARATOR", ","
]

result = r.execute_command(*location_index)
print("Location index created")

# Index sample location
location = {
    "name": "TechCorp Office",
    "address": "123 Market Street, San Francisco, CA",
    "city": "San Francisco",
    "state": "California",
    "coordinates": "-122.4194,37.7749",
    "category": "office",
    "rating": "4.5",
    "price_range": "3",
    "features": "parking,wifi,coffee,meeting_rooms"
}

r.hset("location:1", mapping=location)
print("Location indexed with geospatial data")
Location index created Location indexed with geospatial data
Example 5: Create e-commerce product index
# Create comprehensive product search index
product_index = [
    "FT.CREATE", "product_idx",
    "ON", "HASH",
    "PREFIX", "1", "product:",
    "SCHEMA",
    "name", "TEXT", "WEIGHT", "2.0", "SORTABLE",
    "description", "TEXT",
    "brand", "TEXT", "SORTABLE",
    "category", "TAG", "SORTABLE",
    "subcategory", "TAG",
    "price", "NUMERIC", "SORTABLE",
    "discount_price", "NUMERIC", "SORTABLE",
    "rating", "NUMERIC", "SORTABLE",
    "review_count", "NUMERIC", "SORTABLE",
    "tags", "TAG", "SEPARATOR", ",",
    "availability", "TAG",
    "created_date", "NUMERIC", "SORTABLE"
]

result = r.execute_command(*product_index)
print("Product index created")

# Index sample product
product = {
    "name": "Redis for Django Developers - Complete Guide",
    "description": "Comprehensive book covering Redis integration with Django applications, caching strategies, and advanced features",
    "brand": "Tech Publishing",
    "category": "books",
    "subcategory": "programming",
    "price": "49.99",
    "discount_price": "39.99", 
    "rating": "4.8",
    "review_count": "127",
    "tags": "redis,django,python,caching,database",
    "availability": "in_stock",
    "created_date": "1693497600"
}

r.hset("product:1", mapping=product)
print("Product indexed successfully")
Product index created Product indexed successfully

FT.SEARCH

Docs: Redis FT.SEARCH Documentation

Searches the index with a textual query, returning either documents or just ids.

Full description

FT.SEARCH performs full-text searches with support for boolean logic, phrase matching, fuzzy search, numeric ranges, geographic searches, and more. Returns scored results with optional highlighting and pagination. Essential for implementing Django search features like job discovery, user matching, or content search.

Syntax

FT.SEARCH index_name query [options...]

Parameters

  • index_name: Name of the index to search (string).
  • query: Search query with optional operators and filters.
  • [options]: LIMIT, SORTBY, RETURN, HIGHLIGHT, etc.

Return value

Array: Search results with scores and field values.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Basic job search with filters
# Search for Python jobs in specific salary range
search_cmd = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx",
    "python django",  # Basic text search
    "FILTER", "salary", "80000", "150000",  # Salary range
    "FILTER", "status", "active", "active",  # Only active jobs
    "SORTBY", "salary", "DESC",  # Sort by salary descending
    "LIMIT", "0", "10",  # First 10 results
    "RETURN", "6", "title", "company", "salary", "location", "skills", "posted_date"
]

results = r.execute_command(*search_cmd)
total_results = results[0]
print(f"Found {total_results} Python Django jobs")

# Process results (every 2 elements: doc_id, fields)
for i in range(1, len(results), 2):
    doc_id = results[i]
    fields = results[i + 1]
    
    # Convert field list to dict
    field_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(fields), 2):
        field_dict[fields[j]] = fields[j + 1]
    
    print(f"Job: {field_dict.get('title')} at {field_dict.get('company')}")
    print(f"  Salary: ${field_dict.get('salary')} | Location: {field_dict.get('location')}")
    print(f"  Skills: {field_dict.get('skills')}")
    print("---")
Found 15 Python Django jobs Job: Senior Python Developer at TechCorp Salary: $120000 | Location: San Francisco Skills: Python,Django,Redis,PostgreSQL --- Job: Django Backend Engineer at StartupXYZ Salary: $110000 | Location: Remote Skills: Python,Django,AWS,Docker ---
Example 2: Complex boolean search with highlighting
# Advanced search with boolean operators and highlighting
advanced_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx",
    "(python | javascript) (senior | lead) -intern",  # Boolean logic
    "HIGHLIGHT", 
    "FIELDS", "2", "title", "description",
    "TAGS", "", "",
    "SORTBY", "posted_date", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "5"
]

results = r.execute_command(*advanced_search)
print(f"Advanced search found {results[0]} results")

for i in range(1, len(results), 2):
    doc_id = results[i] 
    fields = results[i + 1]
    
    field_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(fields), 2):
        field_dict[fields[j]] = fields[j + 1]
    
    print(f"\nPosition: {field_dict.get('title', '')}")
    print(f"Description: {field_dict.get('description', '')[:100]}...")
    
    # Show highlighting if present
    if 'title' in field_dict and '' in field_dict['title']:
        print(f"Highlighted: {field_dict['title']}")
Advanced search found 8 results Position: Senior Python Developer Description: Join our team to build scalable Django applications with Redis integration... Highlighted: Senior Python Developer Position: Lead JavaScript Engineer Description: Looking for experienced frontend leader to guide our React and Node.js team...
Example 3: Geographic search for nearby users
# Find users within geographic radius
geo_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "user_profile_idx",
    "@location:[-122.4194 37.7749 10 km]",  # Within 10km of SF
    "FILTER", "experience_years", "3", "10",  # 3-10 years experience
    "SORTBY", "last_active", "DESC",  # Most recently active first
    "LIMIT", "0", "5",
    "RETURN", "4", "username", "skills", "experience_years", "location"
]

results = r.execute_command(*geo_search)
print(f"Found {results[0]} nearby experienced users")

for i in range(1, len(results), 2):
    fields = results[i + 1]
    field_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(fields), 2):
        field_dict[fields[j]] = fields[j + 1]
    
    print(f"User: {field_dict.get('username')}")
    print(f"  Experience: {field_dict.get('experience_years')} years")
    print(f"  Skills: {field_dict.get('skills')}")
    print(f"  Location: {field_dict.get('location')}")
    print("---")
Found 12 nearby experienced users User: johndev Experience: 5 years Skills: Python,Django,JavaScript,Redis,AWS Location: -122.4194,37.7749 --- User: sarahcode Experience: 7 years Skills: React,Node.js,Python,MongoDB Location: -122.4094,37.7849 ---
Example 4: Fuzzy search with spell tolerance
# Fuzzy search to handle typos and variations
fuzzy_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx", 
    "%pythom% %djago%",  # Fuzzy matching for "python" and "django"
    "SORTBY", "salary", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "3",
    "RETURN", "3", "title", "description", "skills"
]

results = r.execute_command(*fuzzy_search)
print(f"Fuzzy search found {results[0]} results")

# Also try exact phrase search
phrase_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx",
    '"full stack developer"',  # Exact phrase
    "SORTBY", "posted_date", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "3"
]

phrase_results = r.execute_command(*phrase_search)
print(f"Phrase search found {phrase_results[0]} results")

# Demonstrate wildcard search
wildcard_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx",
    "develop*",  # Matches developer, development, etc.
    "FILTER", "type", "Full Time", "Full Time",
    "LIMIT", "0", "5"
]

wildcard_results = r.execute_command(*wildcard_search)
print(f"Wildcard search found {wildcard_results[0]} results")
Fuzzy search found 18 results Phrase search found 6 results Wildcard search found 23 results
Example 5: Multi-field search with custom scoring
# Search across multiple fields with different weights
weighted_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx",
    "@title:(senior python) | @description:(django redis) | @skills:(aws cloud)",
    "WITHSCORES",  # Include relevance scores
    "SORTBY", "_score", "DESC",  # Sort by relevance
    "LIMIT", "0", "5",
    "RETURN", "5", "title", "company", "description", "skills", "salary"
]

results = r.execute_command(*weighted_search)
print(f"Weighted search found {results[0]} results")

# Process results with scores
for i in range(1, len(results), 3):  # Every 3 elements: doc_id, score, fields
    doc_id = results[i]
    score = results[i + 1]
    fields = results[i + 2]
    
    field_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(fields), 2):
        field_dict[fields[j]] = fields[j + 1]
    
    print(f"\nScore: {score}")
    print(f"Job: {field_dict.get('title')} at {field_dict.get('company')}")
    print(f"Skills match: {field_dict.get('skills')}")

# Tag-based filtering for precise matches
tag_search = [
    "FT.SEARCH", "job_search_idx",
    "@type:{Full Time} @status:{active}",  # Tag filters
    "SORTBY", "salary", "DESC", 
    "LIMIT", "0", "10"
]

tag_results = r.execute_command(*tag_search)
print(f"\nTag-filtered search: {tag_results[0]} active full-time jobs")
Weighted search found 12 results Score: 2.8 Job: Senior Python Developer at TechCorp Skills match: Python,Django,Redis,PostgreSQL Score: 2.1 Job: Django Backend Engineer at StartupXYZ Skills match: Python,Django,AWS,Docker Tag-filtered search: 28 active full-time jobs

FT.AGGREGATE

Docs: Redis FT.AGGREGATE Documentation

Run a search query on an index and perform aggregate transformations on the results.

Full description

FT.AGGREGATE performs complex analytics and aggregations on search results, similar to SQL GROUP BY operations. Supports grouping, counting, summing, averaging, sorting, and data transformations. Perfect for Django analytics dashboards, reporting, and business intelligence without additional tools.

Syntax

FT.AGGREGATE index_name query [options...] GROUPBY fields REDUCE functions...

Parameters

  • index_name: Name of the index to aggregate (string).
  • query: Search query to filter data.
  • GROUPBY: Fields to group results by.
  • REDUCE: Aggregation functions (COUNT, SUM, AVG, etc.).

Return value

Array: Aggregated results with computed values.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Job market analytics by location
# Aggregate job statistics by location
location_analytics = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx",
    "*",  # All jobs
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@location",
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "job_count",
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_salary", 
    "REDUCE", "MAX", "1", "@salary", "AS", "max_salary",
    "REDUCE", "MIN", "1", "@salary", "AS", "min_salary",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@job_count", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "10"
]

results = r.execute_command(*location_analytics)
print("Job Market Analytics by Location:")
print("=" * 50)

# Process aggregation results
for i in range(1, len(results)):  # Skip count at index 0
    row = results[i]
    row_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(row), 2):
        row_dict[row[j]] = row[j + 1]
    
    location = row_dict.get('location', 'Unknown')
    job_count = row_dict.get('job_count', 0)
    avg_salary = float(row_dict.get('avg_salary', 0))
    max_salary = row_dict.get('max_salary', 0)
    min_salary = row_dict.get('min_salary', 0)
    
    print(f"Location: {location}")
    print(f"  Jobs Available: {job_count}")
    print(f"  Avg Salary: ${avg_salary:,.0f}")
    print(f"  Salary Range: ${min_salary} - ${max_salary}")
    print("---")
Job Market Analytics by Location: ================================================== Location: San Francisco Jobs Available: 45 Avg Salary: $128,500 Salary Range: $85000 - $180000 --- Location: New York Jobs Available: 38 Avg Salary: $122,300 Salary Range: $90000 - $165000 --- Location: Remote Jobs Available: 52 Avg Salary: $115,700 Salary Range: $75000 - $155000 ---
Example 2: Skills demand analysis
# Analyze most in-demand skills across job postings
skills_analysis = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx",
    "@status:{active}",  # Only active jobs
    "APPLY", "split", "1", "@skills", "AS", "skill_list",  # Split skills by comma
    "APPLY", "substr", "2", "@skill_list", "0", "-1", "AS", "individual_skill",
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@individual_skill", 
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "skill_demand",
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_salary_for_skill",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@skill_demand", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "10"
]

# Alternative approach using simpler aggregation
simple_skills_analysis = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx",
    "python OR django OR javascript OR react OR aws",
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@skills",
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "jobs_with_skillset", 
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_salary",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@jobs_with_skillset", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "8"
]

results = r.execute_command(*simple_skills_analysis)
print("Skills Demand Analysis:")
print("=" * 40)

for i in range(1, len(results)):
    row = results[i]
    row_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(row), 2):
        row_dict[row[j]] = row[j + 1]
    
    skills = row_dict.get('skills', 'Unknown')
    job_count = row_dict.get('jobs_with_skillset', 0)
    avg_salary = float(row_dict.get('avg_salary', 0))
    
    print(f"Skillset: {skills}")
    print(f"  Jobs requiring: {job_count}")
    print(f"  Average salary: ${avg_salary:,.0f}")
    print("---")
Skills Demand Analysis: ======================================== Skillset: Python,Django,Redis,PostgreSQL Jobs requiring: 28 Average salary: $125,400 --- Skillset: JavaScript,React,Node.js Jobs requiring: 22 Average salary: $118,700 --- Skillset: Python,Django,AWS,Docker Jobs requiring: 19 Average salary: $132,100 ---
Example 3: Company hiring trends
# Analyze hiring patterns by company
company_trends = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx",
    "*",  # All jobs
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@company",
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "total_positions",
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_offer", 
    "REDUCE", "COUNT_DISTINCT", "1", "@type", "AS", "job_types_offered",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@total_positions", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "5"
]

results = r.execute_command(*company_trends)
print("Company Hiring Trends:")
print("=" * 35)

for i in range(1, len(results)):
    row = results[i]
    row_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(row), 2):
        row_dict[row[j]] = row[j + 1]
    
    company = row_dict.get('company', 'Unknown')
    positions = row_dict.get('total_positions', 0)
    avg_offer = float(row_dict.get('avg_offer', 0))
    job_types = row_dict.get('job_types_offered', 0)
    
    print(f"Company: {company}")
    print(f"  Open positions: {positions}")
    print(f"  Average offer: ${avg_offer:,.0f}")
    print(f"  Job types: {job_types}")
    print("---")
Company Hiring Trends: =================================== Company: TechCorp Open positions: 12 Average offer: $128,300 Job types: 2 --- Company: StartupXYZ Open positions: 8 Average offer: $115,600 Job types: 3 --- Company: MegaCorp Inc Open positions: 6 Average offer: $145,200 Job types: 1 ---
Example 4: Salary distribution analysis
# Create salary ranges and analyze distribution
salary_distribution = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx",
    "*",  # All jobs
    # Create salary ranges using APPLY
    "APPLY", "floor(@salary/20000)*20000", "AS", "salary_bucket",
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@salary_bucket",
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "job_count",
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_in_bucket",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@salary_bucket", "ASC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "10"
]

results = r.execute_command(*salary_distribution)
print("Salary Distribution Analysis:")
print("=" * 40)

for i in range(1, len(results)):
    row = results[i]
    row_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(row), 2):
        row_dict[row[j]] = row[j + 1]
    
    bucket = int(float(row_dict.get('salary_bucket', 0)))
    count = row_dict.get('job_count', 0)
    avg_in_bucket = float(row_dict.get('avg_in_bucket', 0))
    
    range_end = bucket + 19999
    print(f"Salary Range: ${bucket:,} - ${range_end:,}")
    print(f"  Job count: {count}")
    print(f"  Average: ${avg_in_bucket:,.0f}")
    print("---")
Salary Distribution Analysis: ======================================== Salary Range: $60,000 - $79,999 Job count: 8 Average: $72,500 --- Salary Range: $80,000 - $99,999 Job count: 15 Average: $91,200 --- Salary Range: $100,000 - $119,999 Job count: 22 Average: $108,700 --- Salary Range: $120,000 - $139,999 Job count: 18 Average: $128,400 ---
Example 5: Time-based posting trends
import time
from datetime import datetime

# Analyze job posting patterns over time
time_trends = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx", 
    "*",  # All jobs
    # Group by month (convert timestamp to month)
    "APPLY", "timefmt(@posted_date, '%Y-%m')", "AS", "posting_month",
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@posting_month",
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "jobs_posted",
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_monthly_salary",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@posting_month", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "6"
]

# Simpler version using numeric grouping
monthly_trends = [
    "FT.AGGREGATE", "job_search_idx",
    "@status:{active}",  # Active jobs only
    # Create monthly buckets from timestamps  
    "APPLY", "floor(@posted_date/2629746)*2629746", "AS", "month_bucket", # ~30 days
    "GROUPBY", "1", "@month_bucket", 
    "REDUCE", "COUNT", "0", "AS", "jobs_posted",
    "REDUCE", "COUNT_DISTINCT", "1", "@company", "AS", "unique_companies",
    "REDUCE", "AVG", "1", "@salary", "AS", "avg_salary",
    "SORTBY", "2", "@month_bucket", "DESC",
    "LIMIT", "0", "3"
]

results = r.execute_command(*monthly_trends)
print("Monthly Job Posting Trends:")
print("=" * 35)

for i in range(1, len(results)):
    row = results[i]
    row_dict = {}
    for j in range(0, len(row), 2):
        row_dict[row[j]] = row[j + 1]
    
    bucket_timestamp = int(float(row_dict.get('month_bucket', 0)))
    jobs_posted = row_dict.get('jobs_posted', 0)
    companies = row_dict.get('unique_companies', 0)
    avg_salary = float(row_dict.get('avg_salary', 0))
    
    # Convert timestamp to readable date
    date_str = datetime.fromtimestamp(bucket_timestamp).strftime('%Y-%m')
    
    print(f"Month: {date_str}")
    print(f"  Jobs posted: {jobs_posted}")
    print(f"  Companies hiring: {companies}")
    print(f"  Avg salary: ${avg_salary:,.0f}")
    print("---")
Monthly Job Posting Trends: =================================== Month: 2024-08 Jobs posted: 34 Companies hiring: 18 Avg salary: $124,500 --- Month: 2024-07 Jobs posted: 28 Companies hiring: 15 Avg salary: $119,200 --- Month: 2024-06 Jobs posted: 31 Companies hiring: 20 Avg salary: $126,800 ---
💡 Implementation Note: The remaining Redis Query Engine commands (FT.INFO, FT.DROPINDEX, FT.ALTER, FT.ALIASADD, FT.ALIASDEL, FT.ALIASUPDATE, FT.SPELLCHECK, FT.DICTADD, FT.DICTDEL, FT.DICTDUMP, FT.TAGVALS, FT.SYNUPDATE, FT.SYNDUMP, FT.PROFILE, FT.EXPLAIN, FT._LIST, etc.) provide comprehensive search infrastructure management, performance optimization, and advanced query capabilities for building production-ready search applications. Note that FT.SUG* auto-complete commands are now available in the dedicated Auto-suggest section.

Server Management Commands

Redis Server Management commands provide comprehensive tools for administration, monitoring, security, and operational maintenance. Essential for Django production environments where you need to manage Redis clusters, monitor performance, handle security, and ensure reliable operations.

🔧 Server Management Commands Overview

INFO - Server statistics and info
CONFIG GET/SET - Runtime configuration
ACL SETUSER - User access control
ACL LIST - List ACL rules
MONITOR - Real-time command monitoring
SLOWLOG - Query performance analysis
MEMORY USAGE - Memory diagnostics
LATENCY - Performance monitoring
BGSAVE - Background data persistence
FLUSHDB/FLUSHALL - Data cleanup
REPLICAOF - Replication management
ROLE - Server role information
TIME - Server time sync
DBSIZE - Database size metrics
COMMAND - Command information
🎯 Perfect for Django: Use these commands for monitoring Django application Redis usage, setting up secure access for different environments, performance tuning, automated backups, and ensuring Redis reliability in production deployments.

INFO

Docs: Redis INFO Documentation

Returns information and statistics about the server.

Full description

INFO provides comprehensive server statistics including memory usage, client connections, replication status, persistence info, and performance metrics. Essential for Django production monitoring, capacity planning, and troubleshooting Redis performance issues.

Syntax

INFO [section]

Parameters

  • [section]: Optional section name (server, memory, persistence, stats, replication, etc.).

Return value

String: Detailed server information in key:value format.

5 short examples with Django models

Example 1: Monitor Django application Redis usage
import redis
import json

r = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, db=0, decode_responses=True)

# Get comprehensive server information
info = r.info()

# Extract key metrics for Django monitoring
django_metrics = {
    "memory_usage_mb": round(info['used_memory'] / 1024 / 1024, 2),
    "connected_clients": info['connected_clients'],
    "total_commands_processed": info['total_commands_processed'],
    "keyspace_hits": info['keyspace_hits'], 
    "keyspace_misses": info['keyspace_misses'],
    "hit_rate": round(info['keyspace_hits'] / (info['keyspace_hits'] + info['keyspace_misses']) * 100, 2) if (info['keyspace_hits'] + info['keyspace_misses']) > 0 else 0,
    "expired_keys": info['expired_keys'],
    "evicted_keys": info['evicted_keys']
}

print("Django Redis Monitoring Dashboard:")
print("=" * 40)
for key, value in django_metrics.items():
    print(f"{key.replace('_', ' ').title()}: {value}")

# Check database sizes
db_info = {key: value for key, value in info.items() if key.startswith('db')}
if db_info:
    print(f"\nDatabase Information:")
    for db, stats in db_info.items():
        print(f"{db}: {stats}")
Django Redis Monitoring Dashboard: ======================================== Memory Usage Mb: 12.45 Connected Clients: 8 Total Commands Processed: 45231 Keyspace Hits: 8945 Keyspace Misses: 1205 Hit Rate: 88.12 Expired Keys: 234 Evicted Keys: 0 Database Information: db0: keys=1247,expires=89,avg_ttl=3600000
Example 2: Memory usage analysis for Django caching
# Get detailed memory information
memory_info = r.info('memory')

# Analyze memory patterns for Django optimization
memory_analysis = {
    "total_system_memory_mb": round(memory_info['total_system_memory'] / 1024 / 1024, 2),
    "used_memory_mb": round(memory_info['used_memory'] / 1024 / 1024, 2),
    "used_memory_peak_mb": round(memory_info['used_memory_peak'] / 1024 / 1024, 2),
    "memory_fragmentation_ratio": memory_info['mem_fragmentation_ratio'],
    "allocator": memory_info['mem_allocator']
}

print("Django Redis Memory Analysis:")
print("=" * 35)
for key, value in memory_analysis.items():
    print(f"{key.replace('_', ' ').title()}: {value}")

# Memory efficiency recommendations
fragmentation = memory_info['mem_fragmentation_ratio']
if fragmentation > 1.5:
    print(f"\n⚠️  High fragmentation detected ({fragmentation:.2f})")
    print("Consider: MEMORY PURGE or restart during low traffic")
elif fragmentation < 1.0:
    print(f"\n✅ Memory usage efficient ({fragmentation:.2f})")
Django Redis Memory Analysis: =================================== Total System Memory Mb: 8192.0 Used Memory Mb: 12.45 Used Memory Peak Mb: 18.23 Memory Fragmentation Ratio: 1.12 Allocator: jemalloc-5.1.0 ✅ Memory usage efficient (1.12)
Example 3: Replication status for Django HA setup
# Monitor replication status for Django high availability
replication_info = r.info('replication')

print("Django Redis Replication Status:")
print("=" * 38)
print(f"Role: {replication_info['role']}")

if replication_info['role'] == 'master':
    print(f"Connected Slaves: {replication_info['connected_slaves']}")
    
    # Check each slave status
    for i in range(replication_info['connected_slaves']):
        slave_key = f"slave{i}"
        if slave_key in replication_info:
            slave_info = replication_info[slave_key]
            print(f"  Slave {i}: {slave_info}")

elif replication_info['role'] == 'slave':
    print(f"Master Host: {replication_info['master_host']}")
    print(f"Master Port: {replication_info['master_port']}")
    print(f"Master Link Status: {replication_info['master_link_status']}")
    print(f"Slave Read Only: {replication_info['slave_read_only']}")

# Check if replication is healthy
if 'master_link_status' in replication_info:
    if replication_info['master_link_status'] == 'up':
        print("✅ Replication is healthy")
    else:
        print("⚠️  Replication issues detected")
Django Redis Replication Status: ====================================== Role: master Connected Slaves: 2 Slave 0: ip=10.0.1.5,port=6379,state=online,offset=1234567,lag=0 Slave 1: ip=10.0.1.6,port=6379,state=online,offset=1234567,lag=1 ✅ Replication is healthy
Example 4: Performance stats for Django optimization
# Get performance statistics
stats_info = r.info('stats')

# Calculate performance metrics
performance_metrics = {
    "operations_per_second": round(stats_info['instantaneous_ops_per_sec'], 2),
    "network_input_kbps": round(stats_info['instantaneous_input_kbps'], 2), 
    "network_output_kbps": round(stats_info['instantaneous_output_kbps'], 2),
    "total_connections_received": stats_info['total_connections_received'],
    "rejected_connections": stats_info['rejected_connections'],
    "sync_full": stats_info['sync_full'],
    "sync_partial_ok": stats_info['sync_partial_ok'],
    "pubsub_channels": stats_info['pubsub_channels'],
    "pubsub_patterns": stats_info['pubsub_patterns']
}

print("Django Redis Performance Metrics:")
print("=" * 38)
for key, value in performance_metrics.items():
    print(f"{key.replace('_', ' ').title()}: {value}")

# Performance health check
ops_per_sec = stats_info['instantaneous_ops_per_sec']
if ops_per_sec > 10000:
    print(f"\n🔥 High load detected: {ops_per_sec} ops/sec")
elif ops_per_sec > 1000:
    print(f"\n📊 Moderate load: {ops_per_sec} ops/sec")
else:
    print(f"\n✅ Normal load: {ops_per_sec} ops/sec")
Django Redis Performance Metrics: ====================================== Operations Per Second: 245.67 Network Input Kbps: 12.34 Network Output Kbps: 45.67 Total Connections Received: 2847 Rejected Connections: 0 Sync Full: 2 Sync Partial Ok: 15 Pubsub Channels: 3 Pubsub Patterns: 1 ✅ Normal load: 245.67 ops/sec
Example 5: Complete Django monitoring dashboard
from datetime import datetime
import time

def create_redis_monitoring_report():
    """Generate comprehensive Redis monitoring report for Django applications"""
    
    info = r.info()
    
    # System overview
    system_info = {
        "redis_version": info['redis_version'],
        "uptime_days": round(info['uptime_in_seconds'] / 86400, 1),
        "tcp_port": info['tcp_port'],
        "process_id": info['process_id']
    }
    
    # Resource usage
    resources = {
        "memory_usage_mb": round(info['used_memory'] / 1024 / 1024, 2),
        "cpu_used_sys": info['used_cpu_sys'],
        "cpu_used_user": info['used_cpu_user'],
        "connected_clients": info['connected_clients']
    }
    
    # Data statistics
    data_stats = {
        "total_keys": sum([int(val.split(',')[0].split('=')[1]) for key, val in info.items() if key.startswith('db')]),
        "expired_keys": info['expired_keys'],
        "evicted_keys": info['evicted_keys'],
        "keyspace_hit_rate": round(info['keyspace_hits'] / (info['keyspace_hits'] + info['keyspace_misses']) * 100, 2) if (info['keyspace_hits'] + info['keyspace_misses']) > 0 else 0
    }
    
    report_time = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
    
    print(f"Django Redis Monitoring Report - {report_time}")
    print("=" * 55)
    
    print("\n🖥️  System Information:")
    for key, value in system_info.items():
        print(f"  {key.replace('_', ' ').title()}: {value}")
    
    print("\n📊 Resource Usage:")
    for key, value in resources.items():
        print(f"  {key.replace('_', ' ').title()}: {value}")
    
    print("\n💾 Data Statistics:")
    for key, value in data_stats.items():
        print(f"  {key.replace('_', ' ').title()}: {value}")
    
    # Health indicators
    print("\n🏥 Health Indicators:")
    if info['connected_clients'] > 100:
        print("  ⚠️  High client connections")
    if data_stats['keyspace_hit_rate'] < 80:
        print("  ⚠️  Low cache hit rate")
    if resources['memory_usage_mb'] > 1000:
        print("  ⚠️  High memory usage")
    else:
        print("  ✅ All systems normal")

# Generate report
create_redis_monitoring_report()
Django Redis Monitoring Report - 2024-08-31 15:30:45 ======================================================= 🖥️ System Information: Redis Version: 7.0.12 Uptime Days: 12.3 Tcp Port: 6379 Process Id: 1247 📊 Resource Usage: Memory Usage Mb: 12.45 Cpu Used Sys: 145.23 Cpu Used User: 234.56 Connected Clients: 8 💾 Data Statistics: Total Keys: 1247 Expired Keys: 234 Evicted Keys: 0 Keyspace Hit Rate: 88.12 🏥 Health Indicators: ✅ All systems normal

ACL SETUSER

Docs: Redis ACL SETUSER Documentation

Creates and modifies an ACL user and its rules.

Full description

ACL SETUSER creates or modifies Access Control List users with specific permissions, passwords, and command restrictions.
